name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٧١)
```

```english
Most of the former peoples went astray before them, (71)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِم مُّنذِرِينَ (٧٢)
```

```english
and We had certainly sent warners among them. (72)
```

```arabic
فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (٧٣)
```

```english
So observe how was the fate of those who were warned (73)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٧٤)
```

```english
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants! (74)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ نَادَىٰنَا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ ٱلْمُجِيبُونَ (٧٥)
```

```english
Certainly Noah called out to Us, and how well did We respond! (75)
```

```arabic
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٦)
```

```english
We delivered him and his family from their great distress, (76)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥ هُمُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ (٧٧)
```

```english
and made his descendants the survivors, (77)
```

```arabic
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٧٨)
```

```english
and left for him a good name among posterity: (78)
```

```arabic
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٩)
```

```english
‘Peace to Noah, throughout the nations!’ (79)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٠)
```

```english
Thus do We reward the virtuous. (80)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨١)
```

```english
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (81)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٨٢)
```

```english
Then We drowned the rest. (82)
```

```arabic
۞ وَإِنَّ مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ لَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٨٣)
```

```english
Indeed Abraham was among his followers, (83)
```

```arabic
إِذْ جَآءَ رَبَّهُۥ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (٨٤)
```

```english
when he came to his Lord with a sound heart [untainted by sin]. (84)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَاذَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٨٥)
```

```english
When he said to his father and his people, ‘What is it that you are worshiping? (85)
```

```arabic
أَئِفْكًا ءَالِهَةً دُونَ ٱللَّهِ تُرِيدُونَ (٨٦)
```

```english
Is it a lie, gods other than Allah, that you desire? (86)
```

```arabic
فَمَا ظَنُّكُم بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
Then what is your idea about the Lord of all the worlds?’ (87)
```

```arabic
فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِى ٱلنُّجُومِ (٨٨)
```

```english
Then he made an observation of the stars (88)
```

```arabic
فَقَالَ إِنِّى سَقِيمٌ (٨٩)
```

```english
and said, ‘Indeed I am sick!’ (89)
```

```arabic
فَتَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ (٩٠)
```

```english
So they went away leaving him behind. (90)
```

```arabic
فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِهِمْ فَقَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٩١)
```

```english
Then he stole away to their gods and said, ‘Will you not eat? (91)
```

```arabic
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنطِقُونَ (٩٢)
```

```english
Why do you not speak?’ (92)
```

```arabic
فَرَاغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْبًۢا بِٱلْيَمِينِ (٩٣)
```

```english
Then he attacked them, striking forcefully. (93)
```

```arabic
فَأَقْبَلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ (٩٤)
```

```english
They came running towards him. (94)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مَا تَنْحِتُونَ (٩٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘Do you worship what you have yourselves carved, (95)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
when Allah has created you and whatever you make?’ (96)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ لَهُۥ بُنْيَـٰنًا فَأَلْقُوهُ فِى ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٩٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘Build a structure for him and cast him into a huge fire.’ (97)
```

```arabic
فَأَرَادُوا۟ بِهِۦ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ (٩٨)
```

```english
So they sought to outwit him, but We made them the lowermost. (98)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ إِنِّى ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى سَيَهْدِينِ (٩٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘Indeed I am going toward my Lord, who will guide me.’ (99)
```

```arabic
رَبِّ هَبْ لِى مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
‘My Lord! Give me [an heir], one of the righteous.’ (100)
```

```arabic
فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِغُلَـٰمٍ حَلِيمٍ (١٠١)
```

```english
So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son. (101)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ ٱلسَّعْىَ قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ فِى ٱلْمَنَامِ أَنِّىٓ أَذْبَحُكَ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ ٱفْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٠٢)
```

```english
When he was old enough to assist in his endeavour, he said, ‘My son! I see in dreams that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘Father! Do whatever you have been commanded. If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient.’ (102)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّآ أَسْلَمَا وَتَلَّهُۥ لِلْجَبِينِ (١٠٣)
```

```english
So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s will], and he had laid him down on his forehead, (103)
```

```arabic
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ أَن يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ (١٠٤)
```

```english
We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! (104)
```

```arabic
قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ ٱلرُّءْيَآ ۚ إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
You have indeed fulfilled your vision! Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous! (105)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْبَلَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٠٦)
```

```english
This was indeed a manifest test.’ (106)
```

```arabic
وَفَدَيْنَـٰهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٠٧)
```

```english
Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, (107)
```

```arabic
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٠٨)
```

```english
and left for him a good name in posterity: (108)
```

```arabic
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (١٠٩)
```

```english
‘Peace be to Abraham!’ (109)
```

```arabic
كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١١٠)
```

```english
Thus do We reward the virtuous. (110)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١١)
```

```english
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (111)
```

```arabic
وَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِإِسْحَـٰقَ نَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١١٢)
```

```english
And We gave him the good news of [the birth of] Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous. (112)
```

```arabic
وَبَـٰرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَعَلَىٰٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا مُحْسِنٌ وَظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ مُبِينٌ (١١٣)
```

```english
And We blessed him and Isaac. Among their descendants [some] are virtuous, and [some] who manifestly wrong themselves. (113)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a follow-up intended for the previous context, which addressed their polytheism (الشرك), their denial of Allah’s signs (آيات الله), and the threat of painful punishment against them. It states that most of the former peoples went astray just as they have gone astray, and denied the warning messengers just as they have denied them. It cites as evidence the stories of Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Harun, Ilyas, Lut, and Yunus (peace be upon them). The verses quoted contain an allusion to the story of Nuh and a summary of the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** (Most of the ancients had gone astray before them) until His saying: **الْمُخْلَصِينَ** (the devoted ones). This speech is directed toward warning the polytheists of this nation by drawing a parallel between them and the perished nations before them. Indeed, most of those ancients went astray just as these have gone astray, and messengers were sent to them as warners (منذرون) just as a warner was sent to these people. They denied them, and the consequence of their affair was destruction, except for the devoted ones (المخلصين) among them.

The *lam* in **لَقَدْ ضَلَ** is for an oath (القسم), as it is in **لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا**. The first instance of **المنذرين** (the warners) is with a *kasrah* on the *dhal*, referring to the messengers; the second is with a *fathah* on the *dhal*, **المنذرين** (the warned), referring to the ancient nations. As for **إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ** (except the servants of Allah), if the intended meaning is those among the nations who were devoted (المخلصين), then it is a connected exception (استثناء متصل). If it encompasses the prophets, it is a disconnected exception (منقطع), unless the non-prophets are given preponderance over them. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ نادانا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ الْمُجِيبُونَ** (Certainly Noah called out to Us, and how excellent responders were We!). Both *lams* are for an oath, which indicates the utmost care (العناية) given to Nuh’s call and the response of the Exalted. The Exalted has praised Himself in His response, for the implied meaning is: "How excellent responders were We." The plural form of "responders" (المجيبون) is used for magnification (التعظيم). Nuh’s call—as indicated by the context—was his invocation against his people and his seeking of aid from his Lord, as recounted in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَ نُوحٌ رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً** (And Noah said, ‘My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless’) (Nuh: 26), and in His saying, the Exalted: **فَدَعا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ** (So he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘I am overcome, so help [me]!’) (Al-Qamar: 10).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ** (And We delivered him and his family from the great distress). Distress (**الكرب**) — *according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is intense grief (الغم الشديد)*. What is meant by it here is the Flood or the harm from his people. By "his family" (أهله), it means the members of his household and the believers among his people. The Exalted said in Surah Hud: **قُلْنَا احْمِلْ فِيها مِنْ كُلٍّ زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا مَنْ سَبَقَ عَلَيْهِ الْقَوْلُ وَمَنْ آمَنَ** (We said, ‘Carry in it a pair of every kind [of animal], and your family, except those against whom the edict has already been passed, and those who have faith’) (Hud: 40). The term "family" (الأهل), just as it is applied to a man's wife and sons, is applied to everyone who is among his close associates.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْباقِينَ** (And We made his descendants the survivors). That is, the survivors among mankind after their generation. We have discussed this meaning in the story of Nuh in Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ** (And We left for him [a good name] among posterity). What is meant by "leaving" (الترك) is preservation (الإبقاء), and by "posterity" (الآخرين) is the subsequent nations other than the first ones. This sentence is also mentioned after the mention of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in this Surah. In the same story in Surah al-Shu‘ara, it is substituted by his saying: **وَاجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ** (And appoint for me a truthful mention among posterity) (Al-Shu‘ara: 84). We derived from there that the meaning of "truthful mention" (لسان صدق) is that Allah should raise after him those who would uphold his call and invite to his creed, which is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد).

This supports the view that what is meant by "preservation among posterity" is that the Exalted kept alive the call of Nuh (peace be upon him) to monotheism (التوحيد) and his struggle in the way of Allah, era after era and generation after generation, until the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: **سَلامٌ عَلى نُوحٍ فِي الْعالَمِينَ** (Peace be to Noah among all the worlds!). What is meant by "all the worlds" (العالمين) is all of them, as it is a plural defined by the article *al-*, signifying generality (العموم). It appears that what is meant is the worlds of human beings, their nations, and their groups until the Day of Resurrection. For it is a blessed and pure greeting from Allah, gifted to him by the human nations whenever any good occurs among them, whether in belief or action. This is because he (peace be upon him) was the first to rise for the call of monotheism (التوحيد) and the refutation of polytheism (الشرك) and its attendant deeds. He endured the severest trials in that for nearly a thousand years, a feat in which no one shared with him. Thus, he has a share in every good that occurs among them until the Day of Resurrection. There is no greeting of such breadth found in the speech of the Exalted for anyone below him.

It has also been said: What is meant by "all the worlds" (العالمين) are the worlds of the angels and the two heavy ones (الثقلين)—the jinn and mankind.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ** (Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous). This is a justification for the honor bestowed upon him, such as answering his call, delivering him and his family from the great distress, preserving his descendants, leaving for him a mention among posterity, and the peace upon him among all the worlds. The comparison of his reward to the reward of the virtuous (المحسنين) in general is in terms of the principle of a good reward, not in its specific details. Thus, it does not necessitate that everyone shares in what was exclusive to him (peace be upon him), and this is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (He was indeed one of Our faithful servants). This is a justification for his virtue (إحسانه) indicated by the previous sentence. This is because he (peace be upon him), by virtue of being a servant (عبداً) of Allah in the true sense of the word, would neither desire nor do anything except what Allah willed. And by virtue of being among the true believers (المؤمنين), he would not hold any belief except the truth. This permeated all the pillars of his being. Whoever is like that, nothing issues from him except what is good and beautiful; thus, he was among the virtuous (المحسنين).

His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ** (Then We drowned the others). The "then" (ثم) indicates a sequence in the discourse (تراخي كلامي) rather than a temporal sequence (زماني). By "the others" (الآخرين), it means his polytheistic people.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّ مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ لَإِبْراهِيمَ** (Indeed Abraham was among his followers). The followers (**الشيعة**) — *are the people who follow others and walk in their footsteps*. In short, anyone who agrees with another in his way (طريقته) is among his followers (شيعته), whether he preceded or followed him. The Exalted said: **وَحِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ما يَشْتَهُونَ كَما فُعِلَ بِأَشْياعِهِمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ** (A barrier will be set between them and what they desire, as was done with their likes before) (Saba: 54).

The apparent context is that the pronoun in "his followers" (شيعته) refers to Nuh; meaning that Ibrahim was among those who agreed with him in his religion, which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد). It has been said that the pronoun refers to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but there is no evidence for this from the wording.

It has been said: Among the beautiful transitions in the arrangement of the verses is following the story of Nuh (peace be upon him)—who is the second Adam and the father of mankind—with the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), who is the father of the prophets. To him trace the lineages of most prophets after him, and upon his religion rely the living monotheistic religions today, such as the religions of Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Furthermore, Nuh (peace be upon him) was delivered by Allah from drowning, and Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was delivered by Allah from burning.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ جاءَ رَبَّهُ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** (When he came to his Lord with a sound heart). His coming to his Lord is a metaphor for his affirmation of Him and his faith in Him. This is supported by the fact that what is meant by the soundness of the heart (سلامة القلب) is its being devoid of everything that harms affirmation and faith in Allah, the Glorified—such as manifest and hidden polytheism (الشرك), evil character traits, the effects of sins, and any attachment to other than Him that might attract a person and disturb the purity of his orientation toward Him, the Glorified.

From this, it becomes clear that what is meant by the sound heart (القلب السليم) is that which has no attachment to other than the Exalted, as mentioned in the hadith, and it will come, Allah willing, in the upcoming discussion of narrations.

It has been said: What is meant by it is a heart sound from polytheism (الشرك). This can be interpreted in a way that returns to the first meaning. It has also been said: What is meant by it is a sorrowful heart, but this is as you see [unlikely].

The adverbial phrase (الظرف) in the verse is connected to His previous saying: **مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ** (among his followers). In adverbial phrases, that which is not permitted elsewhere is tolerated. It has also been said that it is connected to an implied "Remember" (اذكر).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ ما ذا تَعْبُدُونَ** (When he said to his father and his people, ‘What is it that you worship?’). That is, what thing do you worship? He only asked them about their object of worship while seeing that they worshiped idols out of wonder and astonishment.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَإِفْكاً آلِهَةً دُونَ اللهِ تُرِيدُونَ** (Is it a lie—gods besides Allah—that you desire?). That is, do you intend gods besides Allah as a lie (إفكاً) and a fabrication (افتراء)? He placed "lie" and "gods" first because his concern was focused on that.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِي النُّجُومِ فَقالَ إِنِّي سَقِيمٌ** (Then he took a look at the stars, and said, ‘I am indeed sick!’). There is no doubt that the apparent meaning of the two verses is that his (peace be upon him) informing them that he was sick was linked to his look at the stars and based upon it. His look at the stars was either to determine the hour and the specific time—like one who has a periodic fever and determines its time by the rising of a star, its setting, or a specific position of the stars—or to gain knowledge of future events, which the astrologers believed the celestial positions indicated. The Sabians were excessive in this, and there was a large number of them in his (peace be upon him) time.

According to the first view: When the people of the city wanted to go out altogether for a festival of theirs, he looked at the stars and informed them that he was sick, that the ailment would soon overcome him, and thus he would not be able to go out with them.

According to the second view: He (peace be upon him) looked at the stars at that time with the look of astrologers and informed them that they indicated he would become sick, so it was not within his power to go out with them.

The first of the two views is more appropriate to his (peace be upon him) state, as he was in such a state of sincere monotheism (إخلاص التوحيد) that he did not see any influence for other than the Exalted. We have no strong evidence indicating that he (peace be upon him) was not sick at all during those days. The Quran has reported his informing that he was sick, and the Glorified mentioned shortly before that he came to his Lord with a sound heart (بقلب سليم); therefore, lying or vain speech is not permissible for him.

Others have different views on the two verses, the most plausible of which is that his look at the stars and his informing of sickness were among the double-entendres (المعاريض) in speech. Double-entendres (المعاريض) — *are when a man says something by which he intends one thing, while the listener understands something else*. Perhaps he (peace be upon him) looked at the stars with the look of a monotheist at the handiwork of the Exalted, seeking evidence for the Exalted and His oneness, while they thought he was looking at them with the look of an astrologer to seek evidence for events. Then he said: "I am sick," meaning that sickness would eventually overcome him, for a human being is never free from some sickness or disease in his life, as he said: **وَإِذا مَرِضْتُ فَهو يَشْفِينِ** (and when I get sick, He heals me) (Al-Shu‘ara: 80). They, however, thought he was informing them of his sickness on the day they were to go out for their festival. The factor that made this preferable to him was his concern with turning (الرواغ) toward their idols and breaking them.

However, this view is based on the assumption that he was healthy and not sick on that day, and you have heard that there is no evidence for this. Furthermore, double-entendres are not permissible for prophets because they would remove trust in their speech.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَتَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ** (So they left him, turning their backs). The plural pronoun refers to the people, and the singular pronoun refers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him). That is, they went out of the city and left him behind.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَراغَ إِلى آلِهَتِهِمْ فَقالَ أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ ما لَكُمْ لا تَنْطِقُونَ** (Then he turned to their gods and said, ‘Will you not eat? What is the matter with you that you do not speak?’). Turning (**الروغ والرواغ والروغان**) — *is to deviate and incline*. It is said its origin is to incline to one side to deceive the one who intends him.

In his saying: **أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ** (Will you not eat?), there is support for what was mentioned that the polytheists used to place food near their gods on their festival days.

His saying: **أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ؟ ما لَكُمْ لا تَنْطِقُونَ** (Will you not eat? What is the matter with you that you do not speak?) is his addressing their gods while they were inanimate objects. He knew they were inanimate, neither eating nor speaking, but his intense emotion and rage led him to represent their position as that of rational beings, and then to hold them accountable as rational beings are held accountable, just as is done with criminals.

So he looked at them, and they had bodies in the form of those who nourish themselves and eat, and there was some food near them. He was filled with rage and surged with emotion, so he said: "Will you not eat?" He heard no answer from them, so he said: "What is the matter with you that you do not speak?" while you are gods whom your worshipers claim are rational, powerful, and managers of their affairs. When he heard no sound from them, he turned upon them, striking them with the right hand.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَراغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْباً بِالْيَمِينِ** (Then he turned upon them, striking [them] with the right hand). That is, it followed from that address that he leaned over their gods, striking them with the right hand, or with strength (بِقوة), based on the view that "the right hand" (اليمين) means strength.

The saying of some that "the right hand" (اليمين) means the oath (القسم), and the meaning is that he leaned over them striking because of the oath he had previously made—which was his saying: **تَاللهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنامَكُمْ** (By Allah, I will surely devise a stratagem against your idols) (Al-Anbiya: 57)—is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: **فأَقْبَلُوا إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ** (They came running toward him). Running (**الزف والزفيف**) — *is to hasten in walking*. That is, they came to Ibrahim while they were hastening out of concern for the incident which they suspected he had caused.

In the speech, there is brevity (إيجاز) and omission (حذف) regarding the news of their return to the city, their discovery of what had been done to the idols, their investigation of the matter, and their suspicion of him (peace be upon him), which is mentioned in Surah al-Anbiya.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ أَتَعْبُدُونَ ما تَنْحِتُونَ وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَما تَعْمَلُونَ** (He said, ‘Do you worship what you [yourselves] carve, while Allah has created you and what you make?’). In this, there is brevity and omission regarding the account of his being seized, brought before the eyes of the people, his questioning, and other matters.

The interrogation is for rebuke (التوبيخ), and it also contains an argument for the invalidity of their way. He is saying: That which a human carves with his own hand is not fit to be a lord for the human or an object of worship for him, while Allah, the Glorified, created the human and what he makes. Creation is inseparable from management (التدبير); thus, He is the Lord of the human, and it is foolishness to leave this and worship that.

From this, it has become clear that the most apparent view is that the **ما** in His saying **ما تَنْحِتُونَ** (what you carve) is a relative pronoun (موصولة), and the implied meaning is "that which you carve." Likewise in His saying **وَما تَعْمَلُونَ** (and what you make). Some have allowed that **ما** in it be an infinitive particle (مصدرية), but this is very far-fetched in the first instance.

There is no harm in attributing creation to what the human makes or to his action, because what the human intends and does by way of his choice is intended by Allah, the Glorified, by way of the human's intention and choice. This type of connection of the Will (الإرادة) to the act does not necessitate the invalidity of the influence of the human's will, nor the act's departure from choice and its becoming compelled (مجبراً) upon it; this is evident.

If the intended meaning were the attribution of the creation of their acts to Allah, the Glorified, without a medium—not by way of their will, but by the connection of His Will to the act itself—and it implied compulsion (الجبر), then the statement would be closer to being an excuse for them than a rebuke and condemnation, and the argument would be for them, not against them.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالُوا ابْنُوا لَهُ بُنْياناً فَأَلْقُوهُ فِي الْجَحِيمِ** (They said, ‘Build a structure for him and cast him into the furnace’). Structure (**البنيان**) — *is the infinitive of "to build" (بنى يبني), and what is meant by it is the building itself*. The furnace (**الجحيم**) — *is the fire in its intense blazing*.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَرادُوا بِهِ كَيْداً فَجَعَلْناهُمُ الْأَسْفَلِينَ** (They desired to outwit him, but We made them the lowliest). Stratagem (**الكيد**) — *is a ruse (الحيلة)*, and what is meant is their ruse to destroy him and burn him with fire.

His saying: **فَجَعَلْناهُمُ الْأَسْفَلِينَ** (but We made them the lowliest) is a metaphor for making Ibrahim above them, such that their stratagem did not affect him at all, as the Glorified said: **يا نارُ كُونِي بَرْداً وَسَلاماً عَلى إِبْراهِيمَ** (O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!) (Al-Anbiya: 69).

With this, a chapter from the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) is concluded: his initial rising against the worship of idols, his disputation with their worshipers, and the conclusion of his affair with his being cast into the fire and Allah’s nullification of their stratagem.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَ إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ** (He said, ‘Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me’). This is another chapter from his (peace be upon him) stories, mentioning his resolve to migrate from among his people, his seeking a righteous son from Allah, the response to that, the story of his sacrifice, and the descent of the ransom.

So his saying: **وَقالَ إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى رَبِّي** (He said, ‘Indeed I am going to my Lord’) etc., is like the fulfillment of what he had promised them when addressing Azar: **وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَأَدْعُوا رَبِّي عَسى أَلَّا أَكُونَ بِدُعاءِ رَبِّي شَقِيًّا** (I will keep away from you and what you invoke besides Allah, and I will invoke my Lord. Hopefully, I will not be disappointed in invoking my Lord) (Maryam: 48). From this, it is known that his meaning by "going to his Lord" is going to a place where he could devote himself to His worship and invocation, which is the Holy Land.

The saying of some that the meaning is "I am going to where my Lord commanded me" has no evidence.

Likewise, the saying of some that the meaning is "I am going to meet my Lord when they cast me into the fire, so I shall die and meet my Lord, and He will guide me to Paradise."

In this—as has been said—the end of the verse does not suit it, which is his saying: **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** (My Lord! Give me [a child] from among the righteous), as well as His saying after it: **فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ** (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son).

His saying, the Exalted: **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** (My Lord! Give me [a child] from among the righteous). This is a recounting of Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) prayer and his request for a child; that is, he said: "My Lord! Give me..." etc. He conditioned it by his being from among the righteous.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ** (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son). That is, We gave him the good news that We would provide him with a forbearing son. In this is an indication that he would be male and would reach the stage of youth (الغلمان). The use of "youth" (الغلومة) in his description, even though he reached the stage of manhood, is to indicate his state in which the quality of his perfection and the purity of his essence would appear, which is his forbearance (حلمه) that enabled him to be patient for the sake of Allah, when he said: **يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ** (O my father! Do whatever you are bidden. You will find me, if Allah wills, to be one of the patient).

None of the prophets in the Quran is described with forbearance (الحلم) except this noble prophet in this verse, and his father in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّاهٌ مُنِيبٌ** (Abraham was indeed forbearing, tender-hearted, [and] penitent) (Hud: 75).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ قالَ يا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرى فِي الْمَنامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ فَانْظُرْ ما ذا تَرى** (When he was old enough to assist him, he said, ‘O my son! I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think’) etc. The *fa* at the beginning of the verse is elided (فصيحة), indicating something omitted. The implied meaning is: "When he was born to him, grew up, and reached the age of assisting him." What is meant by "reaching the age of assisting" (بلوغ السعي) is reaching an age where one normally strives for the needs of life, which is the age of adolescence (سن الرهاق). The meaning is: "When the boy reached adolescence, he said to him: 'O my son...'" etc.

His saying: **قالَ يا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرى فِي الْمَنامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ** (he said, ‘O my son! I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you’) is Ibrahim’s vision of sacrificing his son. His saying **إِنِّي أَرى** (I see) indicates the repetition of this vision for him, as in His saying: **وَقالَ الْمَلِكُ إِنِّي أَرى** (And the king said, ‘I see...’) (Yusuf: 43).

His saying: **فَانْظُرْ ما ذا تَرى** (See what you think) is from "opinion" (الرأي) in the sense of belief; that is, "Think about what I have said and determine what your opinion is regarding it." This sentence is evidence that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) understood from his dream that it was a command for him to sacrifice, represented to him in the form of the result of the command. Therefore, he sought his son’s opinion on it, testing him to see how he would respond.

His saying: **قالَ يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ** (He said, ‘O my father! Do whatever you are bidden. You will find me, if Allah wills, to be one of the patient’) is the son’s response. His saying: **يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ** (O my father! Do whatever you are bidden) is an expression of consent to the sacrifice in the form of a command. He said, "Do whatever you are bidden," and did not say, "Sacrifice me," indicating that his father was commanded with an order for which he had nothing but to comply and obey.

His saying: **سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ** (You will find me, if Allah wills, to be one of the patient) is a comforting of his father’s soul, that he would not be distressed by it nor do anything that would stir the father’s grief over his son covered in his blood. He added purity upon purity in his speech when he conditioned his promise of patience with his saying: **إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ** (if Allah wills). Thus, he indicated that his being characterized by this noble quality—namely, patience—was not from himself, nor was its rein in his hand; rather, it is from the gifts and favors of Allah. If He wills, he is clothed in it, and He has the right not to will, and thus strip it from him.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا أَسْلَما وَتَلَّهُ لِلْجَبِينِ** (So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s liberty], and he had laid him down on his forehead). Submission (**الإسلام**) — *is contentment and surrender*. To lay down (**التل**) — *is to throw down*. The forehead (**الجبين**) — *is one of the two sides of the brow*. The *lam* in **لِلْجَبِينِ** is to clarify what the throwing down occurred upon, like His saying: **يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ سُجَّداً** (they fall down on their faces in prostration) (Al-Isra: 107). The meaning is: "When they both surrendered—Ibrahim and his son—to the command of Allah and were content with it, and Ibrahim threw him down on his forehead."

The response to "when" (لما) is omitted, as an allusion to the severity of the calamity and the bitterness of the event.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنادَيْناهُ أَنْ يا إِبْراهِيمُ قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيا** (We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision!’) is conjoined to the omitted response of "when." His saying: **قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيا** (You have indeed fulfilled the vision!) means: "You have brought it to the place of truth, made it true, and complied with the command We ordered you therein." That is, the command therein was a test (امتحانياً), for the compliance of which it is sufficient for the one commanded to be prepared for the act and on the verge of it.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْبَلاءُ الْمُبِينُ** (Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. This was indeed a manifest trial). The indication in "thus" (كذلك) is to the story of the sacrifice insofar as it was a difficult ordeal and a severe trial. The indication in "this" (هذا) is also to it, and it is a justification for the severity of the matter.

The meaning is: "In this manner do We reward the virtuous; We test them with trials that are difficult in appearance but easy in meaning. When they complete the trial, We reward them with the best reward in this world and the Hereafter. That is because what We tried Ibrahim with was indeed the manifest trial (البلاء المبين)."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَفَدَيْناهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ** (Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice). That is, We ransomed his son with a great sacrifice. It was a ram brought by Jibra’il from Allah, the Glorified, as a ransom, according to the reports. What is meant by the greatness of the sacrifice is the greatness of its status by its being from Allah, the Glorified, and it is that with which He ransomed the one to be sacrificed (الذبيح).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ** (And We left for him [a good name] among posterity). The discussion on this has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: **سَلامٌ عَلى إِبْراهِيمَ** (Peace be to Abraham!). A greeting from the Exalted upon him. In the indefiniteness of "peace" (سلام), there is magnification (تفخيم) for it.

His saying, the Exalted: **كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ إِنَّه مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. He was indeed one of Our faithful servants). The interpretation of these two verses has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَبَشَّرْناهُ بِإِسْحاقَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** (And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous). The pronoun refers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him).

Know that this verse, containing the good news of Ishaq, by its occurrence after the previous good news in His saying: **فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ** (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son), followed by His saying: **فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ** (When he was old enough to assist him) until the end of the story, is apparent—almost explicit—or rather explicit, that the one to be sacrificed (الذبيح) was other than Ishaq, and he was Isma‘il (peace be upon him). We have detailed the discussion on this in the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in Surah al-An‘am.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَبارَكْنا عَلَيْهِ وَعَلى إِسْحاقَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِما مُحْسِنٌ وَظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ مُبِينٌ** (And We blessed him and Isaac, and among the descendants of the two there is the virtuous, and [also] those who are manifest wrongdoers to themselves). Blessing (**المباركة**) — *upon something is to place goodness, growth, and stability in it*. That is, We placed in what We gave Ibrahim and Ishaq stable goodness and growth.

It is possible that His saying: **وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِما** (and among the descendants of the two) etc., is a contextual clue that what is meant by His saying: **بارَكْنا** (We blessed) is the giving of blessing and abundance in his children and the children of Ishaq. The rest is clear.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In the *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** (with a sound heart), he said: "The sound heart is that which meets Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, while there is no one in it besides Him."

And in it, he said: "The heart sound from doubt."

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hujr, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'He criticized their gods, then took a look at the stars and said: "I am indeed sick." Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "By Allah, he was not sick, and he did not lie."'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and in some of them: "Ibrahim was not sick and he did not lie—he only meant sick in his religion, seeking [the truth]."

The narrations regarding the story of Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) disputation with his people, his breaking of the idols, and his being cast into the fire have already preceded in the interpretation of Surahs al-An‘am, Maryam, al-Anbiya, and al-Shu‘ara.

In *al-Tawhid*, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a hadith: A man asked him about verses that were ambiguous to him. He said: "I have informed you that for many a thing in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, its interpretation (تأويله) is other than its literal revelation (تنزيله), and it does not resemble the speech of humans—and I shall inform you of a portion of it so that you may be satisfied, if Allah wills."

"Among that is the saying of Ibrahim (peace be upon him): **إِنِّي ذاهِبٌ إِلى رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ** (Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me). His going to his Lord was his turning toward Him in worship, exertion, and proximity to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. Do you not see that its interpretation is other than its literal revelation?"

In it, with its chain of narration from al-Fath ibn Yazid al-Jurjani, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), he said: "O Fath, indeed for Allah there are two wills (إرادتين) and two volitions (مشيئتين): a will of decree (إرادة حتم) and a will of resolve (إرادة عزم). He forbids while He wills that, and He commands while He does not will—have you not seen that He forbade Adam and his wife from eating from the tree while He willed that? Had He not willed it, they would not have eaten; and had they eaten, their desire would have overcome the Will of Allah. And He commanded Ibrahim to sacrifice his son Isma‘il (peace be upon him) and willed that he not sacrifice him—had He not willed that he not sacrifice him, Ibrahim’s will would have overcome the Will of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic." I said: "You have relieved me, may Allah relieve you."

And from *Amali al-Shaykh*, with its chain of narration to Sulayman ibn Yazid, who said: "‘Ali ibn Musa narrated to us, saying: 'My father narrated to me from his father, from Abu Ja‘far, from his father, from his fathers (peace be upon them), who said: "The one to be sacrificed (الذبيح) was Isma‘il (peace be upon him)."'" I say: The like of it is narrated in *al-Majma‘* from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them). There are many other narrations with this content from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). It has occurred in some of their narrations that it was Ishaq, but that is discarded due to its contradiction with the Book.

And from *al-Faqih*: al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the one to be sacrificed (الذبيح), who was he? He said: "Isma‘il, because Allah, the Exalted, mentioned his story in His Book—then He said: **وَبَشَّرْناهُ بِإِسْحاقَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** (And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous)."

I say: This is what preceded in the commentary on the verse, that the verse by its context is apparent, rather explicit, in that.

In *al-Majma‘*, from Ibn Ishaq: "Whenever Ibrahim visited Isma‘il and Hajar, he would be carried on the Buraq. He would set out from Syria in the morning, take a midday nap in Mecca, and return from Mecca to spend the night with his family in Syria. When [Isma‘il] was old enough to assist him, he saw in a dream that he was to sacrifice him. He said to him: 'O my son! Take the rope and the large knife (المدية), then come with us to this mountain pass to gather wood.'"

"When Ibrahim was alone with his son in the mountain pass of Thabir, he informed him of what Allah had mentioned regarding him. He said: 'O my father! Tighten my bonds so that I do not struggle, and keep your clothes away from me so that none of my blood splashes on them—lest my mother see it—and sharpen your blade, and pass the knife quickly over my throat so that it may be easier for me, for death is severe.' Ibrahim said to him: 'What an excellent helper you are, O my son, in the matter of Allah!'"

Then he recounted the story, in which: "Then he bent over him with the knife—and Jibra’il turned the knife onto its back—and he pulled the ram from the direction of Thabir and pulled the boy from under it, and placed the ram in the place of the boy. A call came from the left side of the Khayf Mosque: 'O Ibrahim! You have indeed fulfilled the vision!'"

I say: The narrations regarding the story are many and are not free from variation.

In it: al-‘Ayyashi narrated with his chain of narration from Burayd ibn Mu‘awiyah al-‘Ijli, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'How much time was there between the good news to Ibrahim of Isma‘il and the good news of Ishaq (peace be upon them)?' He said: 'There were five years between the two pieces of good news. Allah, the Glorified, said: **فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ** (So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son), meaning Isma‘il—and it was the first good news with which Allah gave Ibrahim (peace be upon him) tidings of a child.'"
